title: Animal-Based Farmers Market Haul in Costa Rica

chunks.title: Farmers Market Experience
chunks.summary: The video starts with the host visiting a farmers market in Costa Rica and highlights the quality of raw goat's milk he purchases. He discusses the benefits of raw milk, including its association with lower rates of childhood illnesses and allergy-related conditions.

chunks.title: Fruits and Their Nutritional Value
chunks.summary: The host explores the benefits of various fruits available at the market, including plantains and oranges. He emphasizes the health benefits of orange juice and its positive effects on endothelial function and glucose tolerance.

chunks.title: Avoiding Plant-Based Defense Chemicals
chunks.summary: The host expresses his reluctance to consume vegetables, seeds, and grains due to their defense chemicals that can impact health negatively. He explains the risks of consuming plant seeds and their potential for misincorporation into human proteins.
chunks.transcript: vegetables guys no surprises here I'm going to skip these why vegetables are essentially the leaves and the stems and the seeds of plants seeds being seeds nuts grains and beans I'm not a huge fan of seeds seeds nuts grains and beans because these all contain the largest amount of Defense chemicals in fact I haven't talked about this a lot recently but nonprotein amino acids are hugely present in the seeds of plants and they're actually an unripe fruit so what are non-protein amino acids in your body there are 20 amino acids nine of them are essential that get incorporated into proteins but in nature there are hundreds of amino acids that don't get incorporated into human proteins aren't used in the human body plants actually use these amino acids in their seeds as defense chemicals they can get misincorporated into proteins they can get these get misincorporated into proteins and can cause neurod degeneration because of protein misfolding so not a fan of seeds leaves of plants definitely contain defense chemicals and they contain a lot of heavy metals Slants will concentrate heavy metals in their seeds and also in the leaves depending on what's in the soil that's around them so I'm going to skip that stuff from these guys I'm

chunks.title: The Importance of High-Quality Eggs
chunks.summary: In this segment, the host discusses the nutritional benefits of eggs while stressing the importance of sourcing them from free-range chickens that are not fed corn and soy. He shares his personal preference for consuming raw egg yolks mixed with milk.
chunks.transcript: free okay eggs are incredibly nutritious good source of minerals vitamins all kinds of good stuff for humans bioavailable protein but what the chickens eat matters and so when I'm thinking about shopping for eggs I'm thinking about chickens that are predominantly not fed corn and soy chickens that are eating mostly outside bugs worms that kind of stuff truly pasture raised chickens um that's what I'm looking for with egg so you want the highest quality eggs because it matters if the chickens are eating just corn and soy if they're inside the quality of the egg is going to be different so that's what I think about one I'm thing about eggs I might eat one or two eggs a day I do the egg yolks raw mostly it's just easier for me I don't eat the whites raw because the white contains a protein called avidin which binds B6 so if I wanted to eat the whole egg I would just cook the whole egg I find it really convenient and easy to just eat the pure egg yolk sometimes chunks.title: Evaluating Honey Quality
chunks.summary: The host talks about sourcing honey at the farmers market, focusing on its quality by ensuring it is organic and glyphosate-free. He plans to use honey to sweeten his milk and shares its nutritional benefits.

chunks.title: Meat and Organ Selection
chunks.summary: The host visits a local grass-fed butcher, discussing the importance of sourcing quality meat and organs. He explains how different parts of the animal contribute to a balanced diet and his practice of making bone broth.
chunks.transcript: grass-fed butcher I think this is pretty cool that uh even in like small town Costa Rica we've got grass-fed meats and one of the things I like about living in a small community is I can talk to the farm Farmers I can talk to the guys that rais these cows talk about how they're fed are they given antibiotics are they given injections are they just fed all grass and yeah everything checks out and this this meat is great so these guys will have meat and organs all kinds of good stuff let's go check it out so I've got lots of ground beef already at the house today I'm going to get skirt steak which is actually the diaphragm of the animal and a ribeye which looks really good ribeye is one of the cuts near the ribs different part of the cow but I didn't know that there was only one hanger steak on the animal but the skirt is actually the diaph R so I'm going to get those steaks and then we'll see what else I'm going to get here hopefully they have some liver these guys know me well so I can just go back there and get it myself those look pretty good I'm also going to get some bones I use these to make bone broth which I eat basically every day so I think it's important when you're eating meat to balance the amino acids that are high in muscle meat like a ribeye or a skirt with the amino acids that are high in like a bone broth or a collagenous tissue so which amino acids am I talking about uh tryptophan methionine high and muscle meat glycine Proline hydroxyproline high in the bones and the collagen so these balance each other out this is kind of like organs the nutrients in organs whether it's liver or heart are going to balance the nutrients in meat meat is a great source of vitamin B12 vitamin B6 and zinc where organs like liver have folate riboflavin a little more choline bioin vitam a and copper things that aren't as well represented or not at all in the muscle meat so just like I want to eat organs with my muscle meat I want to eat collagenous tissue with my muscle meat what's the idea here it's not rocket science it's eating the whole animal and then you get this fully rounded out nutritional profile I think a lot of the bad rrap that meat gets sometimes is due to people only eating muscle meat that's never the way we would have eaten it in the past again the quality of the meat matters but eating the whole animal as

chunks.title: Health Benefits of Animal Fats
chunks.summary: The segment highlights butter as a nutritious food, debunking myths about saturated fats. The host explains the importance of animal fats for hormone balance and overall health, emphasizing how they provide essential nutrients.
chunks.transcript: the animal fats are so healthy for humans CC acid is a longchain saturated fat that is associated with leanness and health in animal models and in humans it affects mondre in a positive way in humans turns them on essentially there's a study in nature that shows that so that's in butter butter also has fat soluble vitamins vitamin A vitamin E is in butter vitamin K2 there's so many nutrients in butter you just think of butter as fat and makes you fat but it's completely the opposite butter is nutrient-rich food for humans it spels all of these hormonal processes your brain I mean your brain is made of fat your hormones are made of skeletons that are derived from fats and so butter is a health food it's a way to help support your hormones your libido your energy your brain function I always kind of play with my macros a little bit but i' probably say I get 35 40% of my calories from fat now usually it's fat that just occurs in the steaks that I eat but if something is a little bit lean I will add butter to it and a little bit of butter to a steak that's too lean or ground be that's too lean will fix the problem immediately I think saturated fat gets a bad rrap because of epidemiology associational studies but if you really dig into the data this is good for you and you'll feel it I mean there's a reason that butter tastes delicious it's healthy for you it's the

chunks.title: Conclusion of the Grocery Haul
chunks.summary: In the final segment, the host summarizes his grocery haul, which includes various meats, fruits, and dairy. He expresses the satisfaction of sourcing high-quality nutrition from local farmers and the benefits of living in a community that supports sustainable practices.
